title: DVWA Explorer
prerequisites: DVWA installed and running in lab
learning_objectives: Recognise common web vulnerability patterns through hands-on observation
Understand how SQL injection, XSS, and command injection manifest
Compare vulnerability classes and identify common root causes
scenario: You have set up DVWA (Damn Vulnerable Web Application) in your lab. Your task is to systematically explore the first three vulnerability categories at LOW security setting. This is not exploitation; it is understanding what each vulnerability looks like.

scenario_context.setting: DVWA lab at LOW security level
scenario_context.challenge: Observe and document vulnerability behaviour without exploitation
scenario_context.relevance: Understanding what vulnerabilities look like is the first step toward finding and exploiting them. DVWA provides a safe environment to build this pattern recognition.

subtasks.title: SQL Injection Discovery
subtasks.description: Enter various inputs and observe behaviour. Document normal input behaviour, special character responses, and error messages.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Start with normal input (a number, a name). Then try: single quote, double quote, 1 OR 1=1, semicolons. Watch for error messages, different responses, or unexpected data. DO NOT try to extract data — just observe.

subtasks.title: XSS Discovery
subtasks.description: Test basic inputs in the XSS (Reflected) module. Document where input appears in the page and how HTML characters are handled.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Enter plain text first. Then try: test, , "onmouseover="alert(1). Watch if your input appears in the page source. Check if HTML entities are encoded or rendered.
subtasks.evidence_required: XSS observation notes with input/output comparison

subtasks.title: Command Injection Discovery
subtasks.description: Observe how the app uses your input. Document normal function and how the underlying OS command might be constructed.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Enter a normal IP. Then try: 127.0.0.1; ls, 127.0.0.1 && whoami, 127.0.0.1 | id. Watch for additional output appearing. Think about how the app constructs the OS command.
subtasks.evidence_required: Command injection analysis with app behaviour notes

subtasks.title: Vulnerability Pattern Report
subtasks.description: Compare all three types: what makes them similar (user input) and what makes each unique.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Write a comparative analysis: all three take user input and use it unsafely. SQLi injects into database queries, XSS into HTML output, command injection into OS commands. Note the common root cause: insufficient input validation.

reflection_prompt: All three vulnerability types share a common root cause: trusting user input. Design a set of developer guidelines that would prevent all three categories if followed consistently.

quiz.question: The common root cause of SQLi, XSS, and command injection is:
options: Insufficient input validation and unsafe use of user-controlled data
quiz.explanation: All three vulnerability classes result from applications using user-supplied input in dangerous contexts without proper validation or encoding.

quiz.question: DVWA should be run in an isolated lab because:
options: It contains real vulnerabilities that could be exploited if exposed to a network
quiz.explanation: DVWA is intentionally vulnerable. Exposing it on a network-connected machine creates real security risks.

mentor_notes: This is about observation and understanding, not exploitation. Students who demonstrate clear understanding of WHY each vulnerability exists score higher than those who just document what happened.
